questions.prompt: Which sentence uses a three-part phrasal verb correctly?

options.text: I can't put the noise up with.
I can't put up with the noise.
I can't put up the noise with.
I can't with put up the noise.

questions.explanation: Three-part phrasal verbs are always inseparable — the particles stay together immediately after the verb: put up with + object. Splitting the particles, as in the other options, is never grammatical.

questions.prompt: Complete: "She ___ anyone who didn't go to university." (= regards as inferior)
questions.explanation: Look down on means to regard someone as inferior or less important. Look up to means to admire, look after means to care for, and look into means to investigate — three entirely different meanings carried by the particle.

questions.prompt: The phrasal verb in "Turn it down" is separable. Why must the pronoun go between the verb and particle?

options.text: Because pronoun objects must go between the verb and particle in separable phrasal verbs.
Because "turn down" is an inseparable verb.

questions.explanation: When the object of a separable phrasal verb is a pronoun, it must sit between the verb and its particle: turn it down, pick it up, hand it in. Saying "Turn down it" is ungrammatical. This rule does not apply to noun objects, which can go either position.

questions.prompt: Which single-word formal equivalent matches the phrasal verb "do away with"?
questions.explanation: Do away with means to abolish or eliminate something. Postpone matches put off, investigate matches look into, and establish matches set up — each multi-word verb has its own formal Latinate equivalent.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Despite the pressure, he ___ cheating and was never punished." (= escaped without punishment)
questions.explanation: Get away with means to do something wrong and escape punishment. The preposition is fixed: with, not from, of, or on. Choosing the wrong particle produces a different verb or nonsense — the particle is not interchangeable.
